cycles.name: Bedrock
focuses: Re-establish loading capacity around the right hip and knee in pain-free hinge, split-stance and lateral patterns while establishing true working weights.
Carry physio glute-med and trunk work into real gym loading; audit squat-pattern tolerance at bodyweight depth only.

paragraphs: You've spent five months getting back to the court. This block is where we make sure you stay on it — Bedrock rebuilds the loading capacity around your right hip and knee in the patterns they tolerate (hinge, split-stance, lateral) so the joints stop being the thing you plan around.

The mental focus: precision over weight. Week 1 runs one gear below your effort ceiling — you're a lifelong lifter, but the bar hasn't met this version of you yet. Log everything honestly, respect the pain traffic light, and let the numbers climb on their own.
By the end of five weeks: working weights established on every lift, a right leg that matches the left rep-for-rep, and a hip that finishes gym sessions and padel in the same week without complaint.
outcomes: Working weights locked on all 5 primary lifts
Right/left split squat gap closed to ≤1 rep
4 gym days + 2–3 padel sessions coexisting, no flare-ups

cycles.name: Armour Build
focuses: Build hypertrophy and capacity around the hip and knee, progressively expanding range of motion under load as tolerance grows.
Introduce controlled rotation and early deceleration mechanics — the padel patterns, at rehearsal speed.

paragraphs: Bedrock gives the joints their base. What comes next builds on top of it.
Five weeks of putting muscle exactly where your hip and knee need it most, in ranges you'll have earned by then.
The movements start looking more like padel — rotation comes back, deceleration gets rehearsed, and the gym stops being separate from the court.
First we pour the foundation. Then we plate it in armour.

cycles.name: Strength Reclaim
focuses: Build max-strength in the reclaimed patterns, including re-testing a loaded squat variation if the hip now tolerates it.
Build eccentric and deceleration strength for the knee — the ACL's insurance policy.

cycles.name: Power Transfer
focuses: Develop power and reactive strength — jump/land mechanics, lateral bounds, progressive change-of-direction under fatigue.
Build rotational power for the padel swing while maintaining, not chasing, strength.

cycles.name: Court Proof
focuses: Integrate sport-speed — reactive change-of-direction and full-intensity movement prep, with no gym restrictions left unearned.
Lock in permanent hip-durability minimums as a compact, keep-forever maintenance slot.

days.completionMessage: That's the heaviest day of the week banked — hamstrings and glutes loaded, hip untouched by anything it hates. Protein within a couple of hours, and give this one a full night's sleep before you think about the court.

good: Spin light, build a sweat
Let the knee warm into rhythm
bad: No resistance grinding

good: Push the ground away sideways
Burn on the side of the hip
bad: Don't let the knees cave in

good: Drive heels through the floor
Glutes finish the lift, not lower back
bad: Don't arch past flat at the top

good: Sit between your hips, chest tall
Feel where depth stays comfortable — that's today's range
bad: Never push through a hip pinch

good: Press your low back into the floor
Ribs stay down as limbs move
bad: Don't let the back arch off the mat

good: Push the hips back to the wall behind you
Hamstrings load like springs — the bar just hangs
bad: Don't round the lower back to chase depth

good: Drive the bar to the ceiling through your heels
Squeeze the glutes to finish — tuck the pelvis, ribs down
bad: Don't hyperextend the lower back at the top

good: Pull the pad to your glutes
Hamstrings only — hips stay pinned
bad: Don't lift the hips off the pad

good: Tap the heel to the floor like it's glass
Stance-side glute holds the pelvis level
bad: Don't let the knee dive inward

good: Make one straight line from shoulder to knee
Inner thigh holds you up
bad: Don't let the hips sag

days.completionMessage: Every row and face pull today is insurance on your overhead game — a back that pulls hard keeps shoulders healthy through a long season. Easy day tomorrow if the legs need it; the week bends around you.

good: Long smooth strokes
Feel the upper back open
bad: Don't yank with the arms

good: Snap the band to your chest
Shoulder blades squeeze together
bad: Don't shrug up

good: Big slow circles
Chest opens as the band travels
bad: Don't bend the elbows to cheat

good: Push the floor away at the top
Shoulder blades slide around the ribcage
bad: Don't bend the elbows

good: Pull the dumbbells to your hips
Elbows drive back, lats do the work
bad: Don't bounce your chest off the pad

good: Pull the bar to your collarbone
Elbows to back pockets
bad: Don't lean back and heave

good: Press along the bar's angle, tall spine
Down-knee glute squeezed, ribs stacked
bad: Don't flare the ribs to finish the press

good: Pull the rope to your eyebrows
Rear delts and mid-back own it
bad: Don't turn it into a row — keep elbows high

good: Press straight out, fight the pull
Obliques lock the ribs to the pelvis
bad: Don't let the hips twist toward the stack

days.completionMessage: This is the day that does the hip's missing job — every step-up and side plank is structure your socket doesn't give you for free. If the right leg matched the left today, that's the win. Log how it felt.

good: Spin light, build a sweat
Let the knee warm into rhythm
bad: No resistance grinding

good: Open the knee against the band
The muscle behind your hip pocket fires
bad: Don't roll the pelvis backward

good: Drive one heel down, hips square
One glute does all the work
bad: Don't let the pelvis tilt or drop

good: Knee glides past the toes
Stretch low in the calf/ankle, heel down
bad: Don't lift the heel

good: Sit between your hips, chest tall
Feel where depth stays comfortable — that's today's range
bad: Never push through a hip pinch

good: Drop the back knee straight down to the floor
Front quad and glute share the load, torso tall
bad: Don't lean the chest forward to dodge the front leg

good: Step up sideways, drive through the top heel
Outside of the hip hauls you up
bad: Don't bounce off the bottom foot

good: Kick to a full stop at the top
Quad squeezes hard at lockout
bad: Don't swing or let the weight slam down

good: Push the pads apart and hold a beat
Both sides of the hip working
bad: Don't lean forward to fake range

exercises.name: Modified Side Plank with Hip Abduction

good: Lift the top leg while the body stays a plank
Both hip sides fire — bottom holds, top lifts
bad: Don't let the hips drop or roll back

days.completionMessage: Four for four — pressed, carried, and braced like someone rebuilding on purpose. That's a full week of deposits in the bank; the readiness data will tell us how well you cashed it. Go play.

good: Easy building pace
Let the whole body warm through
bad: Don't start at high resistance

good: Move one vertebra at a time
Breathe into the stretch both ways
bad: Don't rush through the middle

good: Snap the band to your chest
Shoulder blades squeeze together
bad: Don't shrug up

good: Push the floor away
Body moves as one plank
bad: Don't let the hips sag

good: Bend the bar and press to the ceiling
Chest loads in the pause, whole body stays tight
bad: Don't bounce the bar off your chest

good: Pull the dumbbell to your hip
Lat drives, arm just connects
bad: Don't rotate the torso to heave it up

good: Thumbs to the sky, arms make a Y
Lower traps lift, neck stays quiet
bad: Don't shrug into your ears

good: Walk tall like the weight isn't there
Opposite-side hip and obliques hold you level
bad: Don't lean away from the weight

good: Press the band away as the legs move
Low back glued to the floor
bad: Don't hold your breath

cards.title: The Pain Traffic Light
cards.body: This is the one rule that runs the whole cycle — for both the knee and the dysplastic hip. What's OK: normal muscle fatigue, the burn of a hard set, general next-day stiffness in the joint. That's tissue adapting, not the joint complaining — keep going. What's not OK: anything sharp, pinching, anything that radiates down the leg, numbness or tingling, or pain that builds through a set instead of just being effort. Use the ladder — mild, and it settles by the next morning: finish the set, log it in your session note. Building, or changing how you move: stop that exercise, drop the range or load, and message me. Sharp, radiating, numb, or unfamiliar: stop the whole session and message me before your next one — don't push through it or wait it out. That loop is how we load a healing knee and a dysplastic hip safely.

cards.title: Right Side Goes First
cards.body: Every single-leg exercise starts on the right — the injured side sets the day's dose. If the right manages 8 clean reps, the left gets 8, even if it could do 12. The 2-rep asymmetry cap in your split squat is deliberate: we're closing the gap, not letting the strong side sprint ahead. Log each side separately so I can watch the gap shrink week to week.

cards.title: Never Stretch That Hip
cards.body: Your hip sockets are shallower than average — that's what the MRI showed. It means your hips likely have more passive motion than they can control, not less. So no deep pigeon poses, no aggressive hip stretching, no yanking into end range, ever — including outside the gym. What that hip needs is strength and control at the edges, and this program feeds it exactly that. If something feels tight, it's asking for activation, not stretching.

cards.title: Padel Is Part of the Program
cards.body: Your 2–3 court sessions count as training load — I see them in your readiness data even if you don't log them. Two rules: never play the day after Lower A (the heavy hinge day), and keep at least one full day between the two lower-body sessions. If a match runs long or turns competitive, treat the next gym day as a low-readiness day and drop every RPE by 1. The goal this cycle is the gym and the court coexisting without the hip complaining.

cards.title: Week 1 Sets the Baseline
cards.body: You know how to lift — but I don't have your numbers yet, and neither does your post-injury body. Week 1 runs everything one effort-notch easier than prescribed: find weights that leave 3–4 clean reps in the tank. That's not caution for caution's sake — those logs become your working weights for the next four weeks and the baseline your whole 24-week roadmap progresses from. Bad week-1 data means five weeks of guessing.

cards.title: Keep the Creatine, Time the Whey
cards.body: Your supplement stack is already right — don't change it. Creatine: 5g daily, every day, gym day or not; consistency is the entire mechanism. Whey: anchor it to training — a serving after each gym session, and on padel days too. Target roughly 1.8–2g of protein per kg per day (about 140–150g at your weight) with the whey filling what food doesn't reach. Healing tissue is expensive — this is how you pay for it. We'll refine in check-ins.

cards.title: Sleep Is Rehab Hours
cards.body: You rated your sleep 7/10 — decent, but tissue repair happens almost entirely while you sleep, and you're asking your body to remodel a knee and stabilize a hip while playing padel. Protect 7.5–8 hours on the nights after Lower A and after matches; those are your two biggest recovery bills. Tell me your usual hours in the first check-in and we'll see whether that 7/10 has room to become the cheapest performance upgrade you own.

cards.title: Log Like a Pro
cards.body: Twenty sessions this cycle, and every one feeds decisions: weight, RPE, and the readiness check before you start. The readiness check matters double for you — it's how I separate a hard padel week from a hip that's actually irritated, and it's what triggers your reduced-dose option on rough days. Minimum effective dose when life happens: warm-up plus the day's first primary lift, done well. That still counts. Silence is the only thing I can't coach.
